perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, and shall take all reasonable precautions to protect the Confidential Information at all times from unauthorized disclosure, publication, or use, including, without limitation, using at least the same degree of care as it employs to protect its own Confidential Information of like nature (but in any event no less than a reasonable degree of care), acting in a manner consistent with its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall endeavor to hold the Confidential Information in confidence, and shall take precautions to protect the Confidential Information from unauthorized disclosure, publication, or use, including using a degree of care it deems suitable for its own information, acting in a manner it finds appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'strict confidence' with 'endeavor to hold in confidence', 'all reasonable precautions' with 'precautions', 'at least the same degree of care' with 'a degree of care it deems suitable', and 'acting in a manner consistent with its obligations' with 'acting in a manner it finds appropriate'. This makes the obligation to protect confidential information vague and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified text weakens this requirement by making the protective measures subjective and less stringent, potentially failing to meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain trade secret status, thereby contradicting CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'strict confidence' and 'all reasonable precautions,' while the changed text uses weaker language like 'endeavor to hold in confidence' and 'precautions'. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain trade secret secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information from a 'strict' standard to one that is merely an 'endeavor,' and changing the standard of care to one deemed suitable by the Receiving Party, the modified text contradicts the requirement to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The change introduces ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce a standard of care consistent with CUTSA's reasonable efforts requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees that disclosure of Confidential Information without the express written permission of Disclosing Party will cause Disclosing Party irreparable harm and that any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party will entitle Disclosing Party to injunctive relief, in addition to any other legal and/or equitable remedies available to it.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information could potentially cause harm to Disclosing Party, and a breach of this Agreement may, at the Disclosing Party's option, justify seeking remedies.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that unauthorized disclosure will cause irreparable harm and entitles the disclosing party to injunctive relief. The modified text weakens this by stating that disclosure 'could potentially cause harm' and that a breach 'may, at the Disclosing Party's option, justify seeking remedies.' This introduces ambiguity and makes the entitlement to injunctive relief discretionary, not guaranteed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California law (Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 526), injunctive relief is available when it appears that a party is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, some act in violation of the rights of another party, which act would produce great or irreparable injury to the plaintiff. The original text aligns with this by asserting irreparable harm. The modified text, by making the harm potential and the remedy discretionary, weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain injunctive relief, potentially contradicting the statutory basis for such relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The original text states that unauthorized disclosure 'will cause irreparable harm' and 'will entitle' the Disclosing Party to injunctive relief. The modified text weakens this by stating that disclosure 'could potentially cause harm' and that a breach 'may...justify seeking remedies'. This introduces ambiguity. Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 526, as indicated in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows injunctive relief when 'great or irreparable injury' would result. The modified text's suggestion of potential harm and discretionary remedies weakens the Disclosing Party's position to obtain injunctive relief, directly contradicting the law's basis for such relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall disseminate Confidential Information only to those employees, independent contractors, advisors, or Affiliates, on a “need to know” basis in order for Receiving Party to carry out the Purpose, and Receiving Party warrants that all such employees, independent contractors, advisors, or Affiliates shall be advised of the confidential nature of the information received and that all such employees, independent contractors, advisors, Affiliates shall be bound in writing by obligations no less stringent than the terms set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party may disseminate Confidential Information to employees, independent contractors, advisors, or Affiliates, as deemed necessary to carry out the Purpose, and Receiving Party will attempt to ensure such individuals are aware of the confidential nature of the information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates dissemination only on a 'need to know' basis and requires written confidentiality agreements. The modified text makes dissemination discretionary ('as deemed necessary') and replaces the mandatory written agreements with a weaker obligation to 'attempt to ensure' awareness, creating ambiguity and potentially inadequate protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By changing 'shall disseminate only on a need to know basis' to 'may disseminate as deemed necessary' and replacing the requirement for written confidentiality agreements with a weaker obligation to 'attempt to ensure' awareness, the modified text potentially falls short of the 'reasonable measures' standard required by CUTSA to maintain trade secret status, particularly as it pertains to employee and contractor access to the information. This contradiction exposes the disclosing party to legal risks.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially contradicting CUTSA's requirement for 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. The original text mandated dissemination only on a 'need to know' basis and required written confidentiality agreements, providing a clear and enforceable standard. The changed text makes dissemination discretionary ('as deemed necessary') and replaces the mandatory written agreements with a weaker obligation to 'attempt to ensure' awareness. This shift could be interpreted as failing to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy, as referenced in snippet 1 which defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The modification introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of confidentiality obligations, increasing the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Immediately upon (a) termination or expiration of this Agreement, (b) the decision by either Party not to enter into the business or scientific relationship contemplated above, or (c) a request by Disclosing Party at any time, Receiving Party will promptly turn over to Disclosing Party, or destroy, all Confidential Information of Disclosing Party and all documents, media, and other tangible materials containing any such Confidential Information and any and all extracts thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon (a) termination or expiration of this Agreement, (b) the decision by either Party not to enter into the business or scientific relationship contemplated above, or (c) a request by Disclosing Party at any time, Receiving Party will make an effort to turn over to Disclosing Party, or appropriately dispose of, all Confidential Information of Disclosing Party and related materials.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires prompt return or destruction of confidential information. The modified text replaces 'will promptly turn over' with 'will make an effort to turn over' and 'destroy' with 'appropriately dispose of,' introducing ambiguity and weakening the obligation to ensure the secure return or destruction of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The modification from a firm requirement to return or destroy confidential information ('will promptly turn over, or destroy') to a weaker obligation to make an 'effort to turn over' or 'appropriately dispose of' may not meet the standard of reasonable measures necessary to protect trade secrets and prevent misappropriation. This vagueness and reduced stringency creates the possibility of trade secrets remaining vulnerable, in contradiction to CUTSA requirements.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting CUTSA's requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The original text mandates prompt return or destruction, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the Receiving Party will 'make an effort to turn over' and 'appropriately dispose of' confidential information. This weaker obligation may not meet the standard of 'reasonable measures' required by trade secret law. The law snippet provided, California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation,' which the modified text could arguably facilitate due to its lack of stringent requirements for handling confidential information. The original text is more aligned with preventing misappropriation.
